Implement the KthLargest class with a min-heap and the k variable.

So the code would be:

class KthLargest {
public:
    KthLargest(int k, vector<int>& nums) {
        this->k = k;
        for (int num : nums) {
            min_heap.push(num);
            if (min_heap.size() > this->k) {
                min_heap.pop();
            }
        }
    }
    
    int add(int val) {
        min_heap.push(val);
        if (min_heap.size() > k) {
            min_heap.pop();
        }
        return min_heap.top();
    }

private:
    priority_queue<int, vector<int>, greater<int>> min_heap;
    int k;
};

solution: 
```cpp
class KthLargest {
public:
    KthLargest(int k, vector<int>& nums) : k(k) {
        for (int num : nums) {
            min_heap.push(num);
            if (min_heap.size() > k) {
                min_heap.pop();
            }
        }
    }
    
    int add(int val) {
        min_heap.push(val);
        if (min_heap.size() > k) {
            min_heap.pop();
        }
        return min_heap.top();
    }

private:
    priority_queue<int, vector<int>, greater<int>> min_heap;
    int k;
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(N log K) for initialization, O(log K) for each add
space complexity: O(K)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses a min-heap of size k to efficiently track the kth largest element. By maintaining only the top k elements, each insertion and query operates in logarithmic time relative to k. The key strength is optimal performance for real-time updates, while the main weakness is that it requires maintaining a heap structure which may have higher constant factors than a sorted list for small k.
